section.title: Joshua Succeeds Moses
section.sectionRationale: As Moses' life draws to its close, he publicly commissions Joshua, entrusts the written law to the priests and future generations, and receives from the LORD Himself both a sobering prophecy of Israel's future unfaithfulness and the command to write a song that will stand as an enduring witness against them.
section.thesis: At a hundred and twenty years old and barred from entering the land, Moses commissions Joshua before all Israel, deposits the written law beside the ark for every future generation, and, warned by the LORD that Israel will one day break covenant, is commanded to compose a song that will testify against them long after he is gone.
section.frameNote: Six movements: Moses announces his age and coming death, and that the LORD, not Moses, goes before Israel; Moses commissions Joshua publicly, 'be strong and courageous'; the law is written down and entrusted to the priests to be read every seven years; the LORD Himself commissions Joshua at the Tent of Meeting and foretells Israel's future unfaithfulness; Moses is commanded to write the Song as a lasting witness; and Moses deposits the law beside the ark and recites the Song.

units.label: Moses' final charge, and Joshua's commission

verses.text: When Moses had finished speaking these words to all Israel,
he said to them, "I am now a hundred and twenty years old; I am no longer able to come and go, and the LORD has said to me, 'You shall not cross the Jordan.'
The LORD your God Himself will cross over ahead of you. He will destroy these nations before you, and you will dispossess them. Joshua will cross ahead of you, as the LORD has said.
And the LORD will do to them as He did to Sihon and Og, the kings of the Amorites, when He destroyed them along with their land.
The LORD will deliver them over to you, and you must do to them exactly as I have commanded you.
Be strong and {{chazaq|courageous}}; do not be afraid or terrified of them, for it is the LORD your God who goes with you; He will never leave you nor forsake you."

Then Moses called for Joshua and said to him in the presence of all Israel, "Be strong and courageous, for you will go with this people into the land that the LORD swore to their fathers to give them, and you shall give it to them as an inheritance.
The LORD Himself goes before you; He will be with you. He will never leave you nor forsake you. Do not be afraid or discouraged."

units.label: The law written, and read every seven years

verses.text: So Moses wrote down this {{torah|law}} and gave it to the priests, the sons of Levi, who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and to all the elders of Israel.
Then Moses commanded them, "At the end of every seven years, at the appointed time in the year of remission of debt, during the Feast of Tabernacles,
when all Israel comes before the LORD your God at the place He will choose, you are to read this law in the hearing of all Israel.
Assemble the people, men, women, children, and the foreigners within your gates, so that they may listen and learn to fear the LORD your God and to follow carefully all the words of this law.
Then their children who do not know the law will listen and learn to fear the LORD your God, as long as you live in the land that you are crossing the Jordan to possess."

Then the LORD said to Moses, "Behold, the time of your death is near. Call Joshua and present yourselves at the Tent of Meeting, so that I may commission him." So Moses and Joshua went and presented themselves at the Tent of Meeting.
Then the LORD appeared at the tent in a pillar of cloud, and the cloud stood over the entrance to the tent.

And the LORD said to Moses, "You will soon rest with your fathers, and these people will rise up and prostitute themselves with the foreign gods of the land they are entering. They will forsake Me and break the covenant I have made with them.

On that day My anger will burn against them, and I will abandon them and hide My face from them, so that they will be consumed, and many troubles and afflictions will befall them. On that day they will say, 'Have not these disasters come upon us because our God is no longer with us?'
And on that day I will surely hide My face because of all the evil they have done by turning to other gods.

units.label: A song as a witness
units.note: so it will not be forgotten

verses.text: Now therefore, write down for yourselves this song and teach it to the Israelites; have them recite it, so that it may be a {{ed|witness}} for Me against them.

When I have brought them into the land that I swore to give their fathers, a land flowing with milk and honey, they will eat their fill and prosper. Then they will turn to other gods and worship them, and they will reject Me and break My covenant.

And when many troubles and afflictions have come upon them, this song will testify against them, because it will not be forgotten from the lips of their descendants. For I know their inclination, even before I bring them into the land that I swore to give them."
So that very day Moses wrote down this song and taught it to the Israelites.
Then the LORD commissioned Joshua son of Nun and said, "Be strong and courageous, for you will bring the Israelites into the land that I swore to give them, and I will be with you."

units.label: The law deposited, and Israel warned again

verses.text: When Moses had finished writing in a book the words of this law from beginning to end,
he gave this command to the Levites who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD:
"Take this Book of the Law and place it beside the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, so that it may remain there as a witness against you.
For I know how rebellious and stiff-necked you are. If you are already rebelling against the LORD while I am still alive, how much more will you rebel after my death!
Assemble before me all the elders of your tribes and all your officers so that I may speak these words in their hearing and call heaven and earth to witness against them.

For I know that after my death you will become utterly corrupt and turn from the path I have commanded you. And in the days to come, disaster will befall you because you will do evil in the sight of the LORD to provoke Him to anger by the work of your hands."
Then Moses recited aloud to the whole assembly of Israel the words of this song from beginning to end:

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, firm, resolute. Repeated to both Israel and Joshua as the charge for facing the conquest ahead, grounded not in human courage but in the LORD's own presence: 'He will never leave you nor forsake you.'

torah.gloss: Law, instruction. Moses writes 'this torah' and gives it to the priests, establishing Scripture itself, written and read publicly, as Israel's ongoing guide even after Moses is gone.

ed.gloss: Witness. The Song Moses is commanded to write and teach Israel will 'be a witness for Me against them,' functioning almost like legal testimony that will outlive Moses and outlast Israel's future unfaithfulness.

basic.context: Now a hundred and twenty years old and barred from entering the land, Moses publicly commissions Joshua as his successor, charging him and all Israel to 'be strong and courageous' because the LORD Himself goes before them. Moses writes down the law and gives it to the priests, to be read publicly every seven years, and deposits it beside the ark as a witness. At the Tent of Meeting, the LORD tells Moses plainly that Israel will one day break the covenant, and commands Moses to compose a song that will testify against Israel long after his own death.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real, historical narrative: an aged leader's public transfer of authority, the establishment of Scripture's ongoing public reading, and God's own sobering, accurate prediction of Israel's future unfaithfulness, later fulfilled throughout Judges through Kings. The command 'be strong and courageous,' though first spoken to a specific leader facing a specific conquest, expresses an enduring truth: courage for God's people is always grounded in His presence, not their own strength.
basic.originalLanguages: Joshua and all Israel are charged to be chazaq, strong and resolute, because the LORD 'will never leave you nor forsake you.' Moses writes this torah, law or instruction, and entrusts it to the priests. And the Song Moses is commanded to compose will function as an ed, witness, testifying against Israel's future unfaithfulness long after Moses is gone.
basic.typology: Moses' commission of Joshua, 'be strong and courageous, for the LORD Himself goes before you,' anticipates the greater commissioning of the church, where the risen Christ likewise promises, 'I am with you always,' as His people go out into the world.
basic.christ: Hebrews 13:5 quotes this very promise, 'I will never leave you nor forsake you,' applying it directly to every believer in Christ, showing that the presence once promised to Joshua for conquering a land is now promised to all who belong to Jesus for every calling He gives.
basic.god: God does not merely leave His people directions and depart; He commissions leaders, provides written revelation to be read aloud generation after generation, and, even knowing His people will fail, provides ahead of time a witness, the Song, ensuring His covenant purposes cannot be undone by human unfaithfulness.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 13:5 applies 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' directly to believers in Christ. Joshua 1:6-9 shows the LORD repeating this same charge to Joshua directly. 2 Timothy 3:16-17 affirms Scripture's ongoing sufficiency for teaching every generation. Nehemiah 8 records a later fulfillment of the public reading of the law this chapter establishes.
basic.oneStory: Moses hands off leadership, entrusts God's written word to every future generation, and even provides for Israel's foreseen unfaithfulness, all showing that the covenant's future does not rest on any one leader's life but on the LORD's own unfailing presence and provision, ultimately secured in Christ.

context.literary: This chapter narrates the transition from Moses' long series of addresses to the closing acts of his life, chapters 31-34, moving from covenant teaching to succession, the Song, and Moses' death.
context.historical: The seven-year public reading of the law at the Feast of Tabernacles, verses 10-13, established a recurring national event that later Jewish tradition, and Nehemiah 8's public reading, would continue to practice.
context.canonical: The charge 'be strong and courageous, for the LORD your God goes with you' is repeated directly to Joshua in Joshua 1 and echoes throughout Scripture as the grounding of covenant courage in God's presence rather than human strength.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ensure an orderly transfer of leadership to Joshua, to establish the ongoing public reading of God's written word for every generation, and to prepare Israel, through the Song, for the reality of their own future covenant unfaithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific historical details, Moses' age, the particular commissioning ceremony, the seven-year reading cycle, describe Israel's unique history; the underlying pattern, that courage flows from God's presence and promise rather than human ability, and that God's people need His written word taught and read across every generation, remains a directly enduring principle.

debates.question: Why does God tell Moses in advance, before Israel even enters the land, that Israel will break the covenant, verses 16-18?

views.label: To demonstrate God's complete sovereignty and foreknowledge
views.case: God's exact foreknowledge of Israel's future unfaithfulness, stated plainly here before it happens, shows nothing catches Him by surprise, and He prepares a remedy, the Song, in advance of the very sin He knows is coming.

views.label: To provide a legal witness that removes any excuse
views.case: The Song is explicitly called a witness 'against them,' functioning almost as courtroom testimony prepared in advance so that when judgment eventually falls, Israel cannot claim they were never warned or that God acted arbitrarily.

debates.bearing: Both purposes work together: God's foreknowledge is total, and His justice is scrupulously fair, providing warning and witness before judgment ever falls.

study.originalLanguages: Joshua and all Israel are charged to be chazaq, strong, verse 6, grounded in the LORD's presence. Moses writes this torah, law, verse 9, and entrusts it to the priests. The Song will function as an ed, witness, verse 19, testifying against Israel's future unfaithfulness.

typology.type: Moses' charge to Joshua, 'be strong and courageous, for the LORD goes before you'
typology.fulfillment: Christ's commission to His church, 'I am with you always, to the end of the age,' Matthew 28:20.

typology.type: The Song commanded as a witness against Israel's future unfaithfulness
typology.fulfillment: Scripture itself functioning as an enduring testimony and warning across every generation, 2 Timothy 3:16.

study.christ: Hebrews 13:5 quotes this very promise, 'I will never leave you nor forsake you,' applying it directly to every believer in Christ, showing that the presence once promised to Joshua for conquering a land is now promised to all who belong to Jesus for every calling He gives.
study.god: God does not merely leave His people directions and depart; He commissions leaders, provides written revelation to be read aloud generation after generation, and, even knowing His people will fail, provides ahead of time a witness, the Song, ensuring His covenant purposes cannot be undone by human unfaithfulness.

crossReferences.note: Applies 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' directly to believers in Christ.
The LORD repeats this same charge to Joshua directly.
Affirms Scripture's ongoing sufficiency for teaching every generation.
Records a later fulfillment of the public reading of the law this chapter establishes.

study.oneStory: Moses hands off leadership, entrusts God's written word to every future generation, and even provides for Israel's foreseen unfaithfulness, all showing that the covenant's future does not rest on any one leader's life but on the LORD's own unfailing presence and provision, ultimately secured in Christ.

quiz.q: Why can Moses not lead Israel across the Jordan, according to verse 2?

options.text: He is a hundred twenty years old and the LORD has told him he shall not cross the Jordan.
options.note: Verse 2 states this explicitly.

options.text: He is physically too weak to walk any further.
options.note: The stated reason is his age and God's specific word, not general physical weakness alone; compare 34:7.

options.text: The people rejected him as their leader.
options.note: The text gives no indication the people rejected Moses; the reason is God's own directive tied to Moses' earlier sin, see 32:51.

options.text: No reason is given.
options.note: Verse 2 gives an explicit reason.

quiz.q: What charge does Moses give to Joshua and all Israel in verse 6?

options.text: Be strong and courageous; do not be afraid, for the LORD your God goes with you and will never leave you nor forsake you.
options.note: Verse 6 states this explicitly.

options.text: Retreat and wait for a better opportunity.
options.note: The charge is the opposite, courage and advance, not retreat.

options.text: Rely entirely on Joshua's own military skill.
options.note: The stated ground for courage is the LORD's presence, not Joshua's skill alone.

options.text: No charge is given.
options.note: Verse 6 gives an explicit charge.

quiz.q: How often was the law to be read publicly to all Israel, according to verses 10-13?

options.text: Every seven years, at the Feast of Tabernacles, during the year of the release of debts.
options.note: Verses 10-11 state this explicitly.

options.text: Every single day without exception.
options.note: The text specifies a seven-year cycle, not daily public reading.

options.text: Only once, at Moses' death.
options.note: The text establishes a recurring cycle, not a one-time reading.

options.text: No such practice is established.
options.note: Verses 10-13 establish this explicit practice.

quiz.q: What does the LORD tell Moses will happen after Israel enters the land, according to verses 16-18?

options.text: Israel will prostitute themselves to foreign gods, forsake the LORD, and break the covenant.
options.note: Verses 16-18 state this explicit prophecy.

options.text: Israel will remain perfectly faithful for all generations.
options.note: The text explicitly predicts the opposite, future unfaithfulness.

options.text: Israel will be immediately destroyed upon entering the land.
options.note: The text predicts future unfaithfulness and its consequences, not immediate destruction upon entry.

options.text: No prediction is given.
options.note: Verses 16-18 give an explicit prediction.

quiz.q: What is the stated purpose of the Song Moses is commanded to write, according to verses 19 and 21?

options.text: To serve as a witness against Israel, so that when hardship comes because of their unfaithfulness, the Song will testify and not be forgotten.
options.note: Verses 19 and 21 state this explicit purpose.

options.text: To entertain the people during long journeys.
options.note: The stated purpose is testimony against future unfaithfulness, not entertainment.

options.text: To replace the written law entirely.
options.note: The Song supplements, and does not replace, the written law described earlier in the chapter.

options.text: No purpose is given for the Song.
options.note: Verses 19 and 21 give an explicit purpose.

questions: Moses charged Joshua to 'be strong and courageous' because the LORD Himself would go before him. What task in front of you right now requires courage that should be grounded in God's presence rather than your own confidence?
God established that His written word be read publicly, again and again, to every generation. How central is Scripture, actually heard or read, to the rhythm of your own life and household?
God told Moses plainly, in advance, that Israel would break the covenant. How does knowing God is never surprised by human failure, including your own, change how you bring your struggles to Him?

The Song was written specifically to serve as a witness against future unfaithfulness. What has God already told you, in Scripture or through others, that you need to let hold you accountable rather than ignore?

Moses' work did not end with his own life; it was carried forward through Joshua, the written law, and the Song. What are you building or passing on that will matter after you are no longer the one leading it?

translationNotes.note: 'He will never leave you nor forsake you' is quoted directly in Hebrews 13:5 and applied to every believer in Christ, extending a promise first spoken to Israel facing conquest to the whole church facing any circumstance.

This establishes the practice, later followed in Nehemiah 8, of publicly reading God's law aloud to the whole assembled community, including women, children, and foreigners, at a fixed recurring interval.

God's prediction that Israel will break the covenant is not fatalism but foreknowledge paired with provision, the Song of chapter 32, given in advance so that when judgment falls it will not surprise, but rather confirm, what God had already made plain.
